These cases for me began in a haphazard manner and I became extremely frustrated that the cases were really long and they really were not satisfying. Alright, so let's get started. Step one, position patient. Sounds a little funny that I'll start with this one, but seriously, it's probably one of the more important parts of the case so that you protect the patient and to prevent external collisions, which obviously is a frustrating part of the case. Step two, we're going to access and insufflate the abdomen by whichever method you are comfortable doing. I typically do an optical entry technique, but you can do really whatever you'd like. Step three, place your ports in whichever port configuration you're comfortable with. I'll link to a video below about my port configuration in extensive detail for low interior section. Step four, we're going to explore the abdomen. Step five, we're going to position bowel. I can't overstress this enough. Don't even look at the robot until the bowel has been correctly positioned in the upper abdomen and you can easily see the sacral pulmonary and the inferior mesentery artery pedicle before you dock the robot. Trust me, it's going to save you a headache. Step six, initiate dissection. A couple ways to do this, we can start up with a medial to lateral dissection technique versus lateral to medial. Now, for the medial to lateral, there are a couple of approaches. You can go infra-IMA first, you can go supra-IMA first, or you can go sub-IMV first. Either one of those are acceptable, and we'll talk about what my preference is in a later video. Or you can do the good old lateral to medial, which is how I trained in residency and fellowship. And in fact, I didn't learn how to do a medial to lateral until I watched every single YouTube video I could possibly find to show me how to do a medial to lateral dissection. which is actually one of the reasons I started this YouTube channel. So actually, let me ask a question now.

In the comment section, let me know what your preferred technique is, whether it's medial-lateral, lateral-medial, infra-IMA approach, supra-IMA approach, or sub-IMV approach. All right, so step seven, we're going to identify and preserve the hypogastric nerves, which leads us to identification and preservation of the left ureter. Step nine, identify, skeletonize, and then divide the IMA pedicle. Step ten, identify, skeletonize, and divide, if appropriate, the inferior mesenteric vein. Step eleven, if needed, we'll mobilize the splenic flexure. And I say if needed because I'm a selective splenic flexure mobilizer. You guys may be a routine splenic flexure mobilizer, and that's okay. I just take it down on an as-needed basis.

For the Sputnik flexural mobilization, there's two ways to do this. Also, a medial approach versus a lateral approach. Step twelve, I then go and mobilize the descending colon and the sigmoid colon all the way down to the level of the parent neural affection, followed by step thirteen, which is circumferential dissection of the rectum. And in this, I vary a little bit from other people. I always go posterior as far down distally as I can go. Then I go anteriorly all the way down to the anal canal or to the pelvic floor, depending on how distal you're trying to go. And then finally I take the lateral stocks last.

Now I know this is a bit different than what most surgeons will do and while there's no wrong way to do this, in a later video I'm gonna cover extensively the reason and the rationale and the execution for this dissection technique. Step fourteen, we're going to divide the rectum, usually by total mesorectal excision, or depending on the pathology, if it's a more proximally located tumor, it can be by tumor-specific mesorectal excision. Step fifteen, we're going to then select a proximal division point. In step sixteen, we're going to divide the mesentery of the descending sigmoid junction and assess the perfusion of the proposed anastomotic site in step seventeen. Step eighteen, we're going to set up for the colorectal anastomosis, and I'll link to a video that goes into exhaustive detail about the different methods by which this anastomosis can be fashioned, whether it's intracorporeal versus extracorporeal. And if you do choose an intracorporeal, you can do a side-to-side, you can do an end-to-side, you can do an end-to-end. Really, the options are kind of endless. Then we go to step nineteen, which is hydrodynamic testing of the anastomosis. Step twenty, if appropriate, the patient will get a diverting loop ileostomy. And finally, with step twenty-one, which is closure of the abdomen.
